title: Standards, Codes, Procedures, and Reporting
narrationScript: Radiography produces two things, an image and a record, and this lesson is about the paper side of the craft, the documents above you and the documents you create. Start by getting four words straight, because they are not interchangeable. A code is a body of requirements adopted by a jurisdiction or a contract, and it carries legal force on that job. Pressure equipment rules, structural welding rules, pipeline rules. The code tells you when radiography is required and what is acceptable. A standard tells you how to do a thing well, and the A S T M standards are the radiographer's how-to library. A specification is the purchase document for a particular job, and it can be tighter than the code, never looser. And a procedure is your employer's written, approved instruction for doing the work in your shop, qualified to satisfy all of the above. Compliance flows up that stack. Your technique sheet satisfies the procedure, the procedure satisfies the standards and code, and the report proves all of it. Now the A S T M toolkit. The guide for radiographic examination, E ninety four, covers technique fundamentals. E one thousand twenty five covers hole type image quality indicators, and E seven forty seven covers wire type, including how to select the right set for the thickness. E one eight one five classifies film systems, which is how your procedure names a film class instead of a brand. The reference radiograph sets we met earlier, E four forty six and its heavy wall siblings, plus the aluminum and investment sets, serve casting evaluation. Digital work has its own family, including computed radiography and digital detector array practices. You do not memorize every clause. You learn which document answers which question, and you keep current copies at hand. Next, acceptable techniques and setups, because the code cares how the image was made. For pipe and vessel welds the menu is fixed. Single wall single image, where the source sits inside or the film does, and the beam crosses one wall. Panoramic, the elegant version, source centered in the pipe, film wrapped all the way around, the full circumference in one exposure. Double wall single image for closed small vessels and pipe you cannot enter, source offset outside so the far weld is viewed through the near wall, and you read only the weld nearest the film, shooting enough stations to cover the circumference. And for small bore, commonly three and a half inches and under, double wall double image, the ellipse technique, source offset from the weld plane so the top and bottom of the weld print as an ellipse, at least two exposures ninety degrees apart, or the superimposed version with at least three exposures at sixty degree intervals. Every technique carries the same obligations. Geometric unsharpness within the code cap. Density in the window, roughly one point eight to four for X ray and two to four for gamma on single film. The right image quality indicator, correctly placed, with the required hole or wire visible. Location markers placed so coverage is provable, with overlap between stations. Your employer procedure pins all of this down. It is qualified and approved, typically by the Level Three, and it controls the essential variables, source type, thickness range, film class, geometry limits, processing. Here is the discipline that matters. You follow the procedure as written. If the job will not let you, you stop and get an approved deviation. A beautiful radiograph made outside the procedure is a nonconforming radiograph. Before any film leaves your bench, run parameter verification like a pilot runs a checklist. Identification on the film matches the part and the report. Location markers present and matching the shot map. Coverage complete with overlap. Density measured with a calibrated densitometer, in all areas of interest, and the densitometer itself checked against a calibrated step tablet. I Q I type, placement, and visibility per procedure. No light letter B printed from backscatter. No artifacts in the area of interest. Then the report. A radiography report typically carries the job and part identification, the procedure and revision, the code, the technique details, source type and size, source to film distance, film class and screens, exposure technique and I Q I data, the density readings, a shot map tying every film to its location markers, the findings for each weld or area with the disposition, accept, reject, repair number, and the interpreter's name, certification level, and date, plus customer review where required. Write it so a stranger could reshoot the job and re-reach your conclusions from the record alone, years later. Films get re-read in laundry rooms, lawsuits, and license renewals. If it is not on the report, it did not happen.
imagePrompt: A Level II radiographer at a steel desk beside a film illuminator fills out an examination report by hand, surrounded by stacked film envelopes, a densitometer with a calibrated step tablet, and a binder of procedures.

sections.heading: Codes, Standards, Specifications, Procedures
sections.body:
Four words that are not interchangeable:

- **Code** — requirements adopted by a jurisdiction or invoked by contract, carrying legal force on the job: pressure-equipment rules, structural welding rules, pipeline rules. The code says **when** RT is required and **what is acceptable**.
- **Standard** — a consensus document on how to do a thing well. The ASTM standards are the radiographer's how-to library, invoked by the code or contract.
- **Specification** — the purchase document for a particular job; it may be **tighter** than the code, never looser.
- **Procedure** — your employer's written, approved instruction for doing the work, qualified to satisfy all of the above.

Compliance flows **up** the stack: the technique sheet satisfies the procedure, the procedure satisfies the standards and the code, and the report proves all of it. Knowing which document answers which question is itself a Level II skill — acceptance criteria live in the code and contract, never in an ASTM how-to standard.

sections.caption: The document stack: what governs the radiographer, in what order, and which direction compliance flows.

sections.heading: The ASTM Toolkit for Radiographers
sections.body:
You do not memorize clauses — you learn which document answers which question and keep current editions at hand:

- **E94** — the guide for radiographic examination: technique fundamentals, exposure, quality factors.
- **E1025** — hole-type IQIs (plaque design, the 1T/2T/4T holes); **E747** — wire-type IQIs, including selecting the set for the thickness.
- **E1815** — film system classification, which is why procedures name a **film class** instead of a brand.
- **E1316** — NDT terminology: the vocabulary your report should speak.
- **E446 / E186 / E280, E155, E192, E505, E689** — the casting reference-radiograph sets from earlier in this module.
- Digital practices for CR and DDA systems govern computed and digital radiography work, with their own image-quality metrics.

Densitometers are verified against a **calibrated step tablet** traceable to a national standard — a verification your procedure schedules and your report should be able to point to.

sections.caption: Quick reference: which document answers which question.
rows: Is RT required here, and what passes?
How do I select and place an IQI?
ASTM E1025 (hole type) / E747 (wire type), as invoked
Film class per ASTM E1815, named in the procedure
How do I grade a casting?
Reference radiograph sets (E446 family, E155, E192)
Exactly how do we shoot it in this shop?
What was actually done and found?
The radiography report and shot map

sections.heading: Acceptable Techniques and Setups
sections.body:
The code cares how the image was made, and for circumferential welds the menu is fixed:

- **SWSI (single-wall single-image)** — beam crosses one wall; the **panoramic** version centers the source in the pipe with film wrapped around the full circumference: one exposure, uniform geometry.
- **DWSI (double-wall single-image)** — for pipe you cannot enter: source offset outside, film opposite, and you **read only the weld nearest the film**, shooting enough stations for full coverage.
- **DWDI (double-wall double-image)** — small-bore, commonly **OD 3.5 in. and under**: offset the source from the weld plane so the weld prints as an **ellipse**, with at least **two exposures 90 degrees apart**; or superimposed, with at least **three exposures at 60-degree intervals**.

Every technique carries the same obligations: geometric unsharpness within the code cap, density in the window (≈ **1.8–4.0 X-ray / 2.0–4.0 gamma**, single film), the right IQI correctly placed with the required hole or wire visible, and **location markers** placed so coverage — with overlap between stations — can be proven from the films alone.

sections.heading: Employer Procedures and Parameter Verification
sections.body:
The employer's RT procedure — qualified and approved, typically by the Level III — pins down the **essential variables**: source type, thickness range, film class, geometry limits, processing, IQI selection. The discipline that matters: **follow it as written**. If the job will not allow it, stop and obtain an approved deviation before shooting. A beautiful radiograph made outside the procedure is a nonconforming radiograph.

Before film leaves the bench, run **parameter verification** like a pilot's checklist:

- Film identification matches the part, the traveler, and the report
- Location markers present and matching the shot map; coverage complete with overlap
- Density measured with a **calibrated densitometer** in all areas of interest — and the densitometer itself checked against a **calibrated step tablet**
- IQI type, placement, and required hole or wire visibility per procedure
- No light letter **B** image from backscatter; no artifacts in the area of interest

Only when every line passes does interpretation begin.

sections.caption: Anatomy of a radiography report: traceability, reproducible technique data, findings tied to locations, and a signed disposition.

sections.heading: The Radiography Report
sections.body:
The report is the deliverable — the films are evidence, the report is the verdict, and together they form a legal record that may be re-read decades later. A complete report typically carries:

- **Traceability** — customer, job, part and weld identification, date, governing code, procedure and revision
- **Technique data** — source type and size (or kV and mA), source-to-film distance, technique (SWSI / DWSI / DWDI), film class, screens, exposure
- **Image quality data** — IQI type and identity, placement, required indicator achieved, density readings per film
- **Shot map** — every film tied to its location markers so any future reader can place every image on the part
- **Findings and disposition** — per weld or area: accept, reject with the discontinuity identified, repair numbers for re-shoots
- **Signatures** — interpreter's name, certification level, and date; customer or third-party review where required

The test of a good report: a stranger could reproduce the shot and re-reach your conclusion from the record alone. If it is not on the report, it did not happen.

sections.title: Procedure compliance is image quality
sections.body: Density windows, IQI visibility, markers, and coverage are not paperwork after the fact — they are what makes the film **mean** something. Verify parameters before interpreting, document deviations before shooting, and sign only what the record supports.

figures.title: The document stack: who governs what

interactive.title: Sign-Off: The Last Set of Eyes
interactive.intro: End of shift on a piping job. A stack of films and a half-finished report wait on your bench, and you are the Level II whose signature goes at the bottom. Work the checklist.

steps.prompt: The densitometer reads 1.7 through the weld area of interest on an Ir-192 exposure; the adjacent parent metal reads 2.4. The image 'looks fine' on the illuminator. What now?
options: Accept it — the eye is the final judge and the image is clear
Reject the film and re-shoot: 1.7 is below the typical 2.0 minimum for gamma radiography in the area of interest
Accept it but note the low density in the remarks column
Turn up the illuminator and re-check before accepting
steps.feedback: The density window is a validity requirement, not a preference — below minimum, contrast in the area of interest is unproven and the film cannot demonstrate the required sensitivity. Gamma single-film viewing typically requires 2.0 to 4.0. Re-shoot.

steps.prompt: The procedure calls for a wire IQI, set B, source side. The film shows a hole-type plaque, film side, with no lead letter F. The 2T hole is clearly visible. Disposition of the film?
options: Accept — demonstrated sensitivity is demonstrated sensitivity
Accept if the density is also in range
Reject and re-shoot: IQI type, placement, and the film-side lead F are procedure parameters, and this film fails parameter verification
Ask the welder whether the joint was accessible from the source side
steps.feedback: IQI selection and placement are essential parameters of the approved procedure. A different IQI on the wrong side, without the required film-side marking, is a nonconforming technique no matter how good the image looks — unless a deviation was approved in writing before the shot.

steps.prompt: Station 4-to-8 film shows a fine dark wandering line near station 6 — but the location markers for that station do not appear on the film. The shot map says they were placed. What goes on the report?
options: Report a crack at approximately station 6 based on the shot map
Nothing — without markers the film proves nothing either way, so discard it quietly
Reject the film for missing location markers and re-shoot the station; the suspected crack cannot be located or its coverage proven without them
Report the line as a film artifact since its position cannot be confirmed
steps.feedback: Location markers are what tie an image to a place on the part — without them coverage is unprovable and a repair crew cannot find the defect. The film fails parameter verification and the station is re-shot; the suspected crack makes the re-shoot urgent, not optional.

steps.prompt: A slag line measures just over the code's length limit for this wall thickness. The site manager, behind schedule, asks you to 'call it borderline and round down.' Your move as the certified Level II?
options: Round down — measurement uncertainty cuts both ways
Record the measured length, reject per the code table, and escalate to the Level III and the customer representative
Accept it but photograph the film as personal protection
Defer the call to the site manager since schedule is an engineering input
steps.feedback: The acceptance table belongs to the code and the contract; the measurement belongs on the report exactly as read. Your certification exists so that the record stays honest under exactly this pressure — document, reject, escalate.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly separates a code from a standard?
options: A code describes how to perform a task well; a standard carries legal force
A code carries contractual or legal force and sets acceptance criteria; a standard such as an ASTM document tells you how to perform the work and is invoked by the code
Codes apply only to castings, standards only to welds
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable in reports
checkQuestions.explanation: The code or contract governs when RT is required and what passes; ASTM standards are the invoked how-to documents — acceptance criteria never live in the how-to standard.

checkQuestions.question: A weld in 2.875-in. OD piping must be radiographed without access to the bore. The expected technique is:
options: Panoramic SWSI with the source centered in the pipe
DWSI with the source offset and the far weld read through the near wall
DWDI ellipse — OD is under 3.5 in., so offset the source and make at least two exposures 90 degrees apart
A single DWDI superimposed exposure
checkQuestions.explanation: Small-bore pipe, commonly 3.5 in. OD and under, is shot double-wall double-image: the ellipse technique requires a minimum of two exposures 90 degrees apart (superimposed requires at least three at 60-degree intervals).

checkQuestions.question: Your densitometer must itself be verified. Against what?
options: A second densitometer of the same model
A calibrated step tablet (density strip) traceable to a national standard
The shim under the IQI
A fresh sheet of unexposed film
checkQuestions.explanation: Density readings are only as good as the instrument, so procedures schedule densitometer verification against a calibrated step tablet with traceable density values.

checkQuestions.question: Mid-job, the specified source-side IQI placement is physically impossible on a closed vessel. The procedure does not address it. The correct action is:
options: Place the IQI film-side with a lead letter F and obtain documented approval per the procedure's deviation process before shooting
Shoot without an IQI and note it later
Place the IQI source-side on a different, accessible weld instead
Proceed however the crew prefers — placement is a technique detail
checkQuestions.explanation: Film-side placement with a lead F is the recognized alternative when source-side is impossible, but using it is a procedure deviation that must be approved and documented before the exposure — not improvised.

checkQuestions.question: Years later, a re-reader must place every film on the part and reproduce the technique. Which report elements make that possible?
options: The interpreter's signature alone
The shot map tying films to location markers, plus recorded technique data: source, SFD, technique type, film class, and IQI data
The film count and total exposure time
The customer's purchase order number
checkQuestions.explanation: Traceability lives in the shot map and the recorded technique parameters — the test of a complete report is that a stranger could reproduce the shot and re-reach the conclusion from the record alone.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course, IE 5.1–5.5
ASTM E94, E1025, E747, E1815, E1316 and the reference-radiograph sets (general reference)
Employer written practice and procedure requirements under SNT-TC-1A style programs (general reference)
